question_title: Will the White House announce at least one new nomination to a Cabinet-level office in April 2026?
summary: The question asks whether the White House will announce at least one new Cabinet-level nomination on its Nominations & Appointments page in April 2026. The current situation is highly turbulent: Trump fired Kristi Noem from DHS in March and nominated Markwayne Mullin (a March event, not April). Labor Secretary Chavez-DeRemer faces a deepening OIG investigation with serious allegations. The Iran war has caused sub-Cabinet resignations. However, there is no specific confirmed trigger for an April Cabinet change. The base rate from Trump's first term is ~15% per month for new Cabinet nominations. The elevated turbulence roughly doubles this to ~30-40%. I estimate 40% probability, reflecting genuine uncertainty - the environment is ripe for changes but no specific April event is confirmed.

factors.description: Trump administration is in high turbulence period (Iran war, government shutdown, first Cabinet firing already occurred in March)
Labor Secretary Chavez-DeRemer under deepening OIG investigation with serious allegations (inappropriate relationship, drinking, hostile workplace) - could be next Cabinet departure
Historical base rate from Trump first term: ~15% of months had a new Cabinet nomination, suggesting most months do NOT have one
No specific confirmed trigger for an April Cabinet change - all evidence points to possibilities, not certainties
Status quo persistence: most Cabinet members keep their jobs even under pressure, and Trump may prefer acting officials over formal nominations
Iran war-related resignations possible at Cabinet level (Joe Kent resigned from sub-Cabinet NCTC post, others could follow)
If Mullin DHS confirmation fails (Rand Paul opposed), new nominee needed - though confirmation is likely
Midterm election positioning and desire to show competence could drive Cabinet reshuffles

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.40 (40%)**

**Key reasoning:**

- **Turbulent environment**: The Trump administration faces an active Iran war, a government shutdown, and already fired its first Cabinet secretary (Noem) in March 2026. This significantly elevates turnover risk above baseline.

- **Labor Secretary at risk**: Chavez-DeRemer faces a deepening OIG investigation with allegations of inappropriate relationships, drinking on the job, and travel fraud. Three aides have been removed. She's the most likely next Cabinet departure, though the White House has stood by her so far.

- **Base rate anchor**: Only ~15% of months in Trump's first term had a new Cabinet nomination. Even with elevated turbulence, most months still don't see Cabinet-level nominations.

- **No confirmed April trigger**: All current risk factors are possibilities, not certainties. The DHS nomination (Mullin) was announced in March, not April. No specific event is confirmed to drive an April nomination.

- **Balancing act**: The ~40% estimate roughly doubles the base rate to account for the elevated risk environment, while acknowledging that the most likely outcome is still that no new Cabinet nomination occurs in any specific month.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the White House posts at least one announcement of a nomination to a Cabinet-level office on its Nominations & Appointments page at https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/nominations-appointments/ in April 2026. Otherwise, it resolves to no.
fine_print: Count only nominations to Cabinet secretary posts or other offices ordinarily treated as Cabinet-level. Re-announcements of the same nominee do not count. Acting designations do not count unless they are formal nominations. Candidate Sources: White House Presidential Actions archive at https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/ ; Senate nominations portal at https://www.senate.gov/legislative/nominations_new.htm.
